title: Input to the Structure Editor

Input to the Structure Editor

Input to the Structure Editor

For every structure editor action, there is an event which causes it. The event is an input to the structure editor and can come from user actions, asynchronous messages, ProcScript instructions, and intelligent behavior in the structure editor itself.

User Input

All input from outside, whether it is user input or an asynchronous message received from another component, reaches the structure editor via an event input queue.

Initially, the application routes keystrokes and mouse clicks through a keyboard translation table. If the input matches an entry in the translation table, it is translated to logical input that the structure editor can understand. This logical input enters the event input queue in the structure editor; when the input reaches the front of the input queue, it is processed by the structure editor.

Input from ProcScript

If the structure editor activates a trigger containing ProcScript, the ProcScript interpreter executes the ProcScript and then returns control to the structure editor, which performs any remaining tasks given it by the ProcScript. When the structure editor has completed all its tasks, control returns to the application user and the structure editor waits for the next input.

The ProcScript interpreter guides the structure editor on the basis of the ProcScript statements it interprets. This is another type of input to the structure editor.

Input from Intelligent Behavior in the Structure Editor

Events that cause the structure editor to work are registered inside the structure editor with Boolean flags—some of which, such as $occmod, you can check in ProcScript code—and, when the time is right, the structure editor starts working. These flags enable the structure editor to know which events have happened and therefore, which triggers to activate.

Thus, if a user enters data in an empty primary key field and then tries to leave the modified primary key, the following events occur:

  • The structure editor activates the field-level startModification trigger the moment that the first character is entered. In this trigger, you can code a script module to check the validity of the data entry (for example, you can check for the first character entered).
  • The entered data is checked against the data type restrictions to prevent, for example, characters being entered in numeric fields.
  • The structure editor sets flags so that it knows that the field-level validate, validateKey, leaveModifiedKey, entity-level validate, and the valueChanged triggers also have to be activated, because the user is modifying a primary key field and an occurrence.
  • When the user tries to leave the field, using the ^NEXT_OCC function to the next occurrence, the structure editor carries out syntax checks on the data in the field. If a syntax definition does not exist for a field, the default syntax restriction for each data type is applied, such as only digits being allowed in numeric fields, only valid dates in date fields, and so on.
  • If the field complies with the syntax definition, the structure editor activates the field's validate trigger, if needed.
  • If validation succeeds, the loseFocus trigger for that field is activated.
  • If the user is allowed to leave the field, the structure editor activates the validateKey trigger.
  • If validation of the key succeeds, the structure editor activates the leaveModifiedKey trigger.
  • If the leaveModifiedKey trigger completes successfully, the structure editor activates the entity-level validate trigger.
  • If validation of the occurrence succeeds, the structure editor activates the occurrence's leaveModified trigger.

As a general guideline, when a sequence of processing is set up like this by one or more events, the structure editor always activates the most immediate trigger first (the trigger associated with the most recent event in the chain). So, if a user enters data in an empty primary key field, then tries to leave the occurrence with ^NEXT_OCC, the structure editor activates the field-level validate trigger first.

Note: The specific data validation triggers (validateKey and validateKey) are important. The structure editor always carries out data type, syntax, and ValRep checks before activating these triggers. For more information, see Data Validation.

After the immediate level, the structure editor generally goes to the lowest level then works up to the highest level. In the example shown in the figure, a primary key is a subset of an occurrence, so the leaveModifiedKey trigger is at a lower level than the leaveModified trigger.

If the user has left the field with the ^NEXT_OCC function or equivalent mouse action, the structure editor knows it also has to give focus to the next occurrence (if there is a next occurrence). Accordingly, the last trigger to be activated in this chain of events is the getFocus trigger.

For any given event there is a finite series of effects, and for each of these effects there is a finite series of other effects. For more information see the detailed descriptions of triggers and ProcScript statements.

Related Links
  • Triggers: System

    System triggers are script modules that are synchronously executed in response to system events that are initiated by commands. System trigger names are predetermined and case-insensitive.

  • ProcScript: Statements

    The Uniface ProcScript language consists of a series of ProcScript statements that cause Uniface to perform specified actions, such as running a form, retrieving data, locking an occurrence, calling another ProcScript module, and so on.
